extra.reasoning: The property requires that (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b) is never jointly true. rec_drug_a can only be set true by r3, which requires (g6pd_deficiency AND diabetes AND low_ejection_fraction); no other rule writes rec_drug_a, and the default leaves it false. Therefore rec_drug_a=true ONLY in the two input combinations where that triple condition holds (hyperkalemia free to vary, since it only affects rec_drug_c via r4). In exactly those combinations, r1 (priority 90, same condition) sets rec_drug_b:=false and r2 (priority 50) sets rec_drug_b:=true; per the per-output highest-priority-wins rule, r1 (90) beats r2 (50), so rec_drug_b resolves to false. Thus whenever rec_drug_a=true, rec_drug_b=false, and the conjunction is false. In all other 14 input combinations r3 does not fire, so rec_drug_a defaults to false, again making the conjunction false. Enumerating all 16 inputs confirms no violation. The high-priority r1 was designed precisely to suppress drug b in the case where drug a is recommended. The property holds across the entire input space.
